fact: The German army fought approximately five months to take Stalingrad (roughly August 1942 – February 1943)
Some buildings changed hands multiple times a day during the urban fighting
source: Stalingrad, Antony Beevor (1998) — describes Pavlov's House and other strongpoints changing hands repeatedly

fact: Temperatures dropped to around minus 30°C during the winter of 1942–43 at Stalingrad
source: Widely documented; detailed in Stalingrad: The Fateful Siege, Antony Beevor (1998), and German 6th Army war diaries

fact: Rations for encircled German troops dropped to starvation levels — as low as 50–100 grams of bread per day
source: Stalingrad, Antony Beevor (1998); also documented in the German Federal Military Archive (Bundesarchiv-Militärarchiv)

fact: Of roughly 300,000 German soldiers encircled, approximately 91,000 were captured and only about 5,000–6,000 survived Soviet captivity to return home
source: Stalingrad, Antony Beevor (1998); figures corroborated by the German Red Cross tracing service and Soviet archival records

fact: Hitler refused to allow the 6th Army to attempt a breakout or retreat from the encirclement
source: Hitler's order to hold at all costs is documented in the war diary of the 6th Army and in Stalingrad, Antony Beevor (1998)
